drugInteractions: Diuretics (furosemide, hydrochlorothiazide): additive potassium-depleting diuretic effects; hypokalemia risk
Lithium: increased renal clearance reduces lithium levels; monitor therapeutic levels
Antidiabetic agents: modest hypoglycemic activity may be additive with insulin or oral agents
Cardiac glycosides (digoxin): potassium depletion from diuresis increases digoxin toxicity risk

name: Gotu Kola
nativeRange: Widespread throughout tropical and subtropical Asia: India, Sri Lanka, China, Malaysia, Indonesia, Australia; also naturalized in tropical Africa, the Caribbean, and South America
habitat: Moist, shaded environments: streambanks, forest margins, rice paddies, roadsides, and grasslands in tropical and subtropical lowlands; adapts to a wide range of soils but prefers rich, moist conditions

description.overall appearance: A small, creeping, stoloniferous herb with rounded, kidney-shaped leaves growing in loose mats 5–15 cm tall. The plant spreads by surface runners (stolons) with roots at each node, forming dense ground-cover colonies in moist environments.
description.roots: Short, fibrous roots at each stolon node; cream-colored; mild aromatic scent; primary medicinal preparation includes roots with aerial parts
description.stem: Prostrate stolons running along the soil surface, 15–80 cm long; slender, green to slightly reddish; rooting at nodes; erect peduncles bear flowers and leaves
description.leaves: Reniform (kidney-shaped) to orbicular, 1–5 cm across; margins crenate; long petioles 2–8 cm; bright green; smooth; prominently veined; emerge in clusters from stolon nodes
description.flowers: Tiny, pale pink to white, in small, compact umbels of 3–4 flowers on short peduncles; individual flowers 1–2 mm; blooms year-round in tropical conditions
description.fruits/seeds: Small, compressed schizocarps 3–5 mm wide; 7–9 prominent ribs; hard shell; brown when ripe

compounds: Asiaticoside (primary triterpene saponin, ~40% of triterpene fraction) — wound healing and neuroprotective
Asiatic acid and madecassic acid (aglycones of the above saponins)
Brahmoside and brahminoside (minor saponins with mild CNS activity)
Volatile oil: beta-caryophyllene, trans-beta-farnesene, germacrene D
traditionalUses: Ayurveda (mandukparni/brahmi): one of the primary rasayana herbs for memory, intellect, and longevity; used in Chyawanprash and numerous formulas
Siddha medicine (Tamil Nadu): fresh leaf juice as a cognitive tonic and anti-aging remedy; known as 'vallarai'
Sri Lankan traditional medicine: eaten as a daily leafy vegetable (gotu kola mallum salad) for cognitive health and longevity
Indonesian Jamu tradition: leaves in beverage preparations for wound healing and skin clarity
Chinese medicine (ji xue cao): treatment of wounds, hepatitis, and urinary infections
Thai traditional medicine: topical and internal use for leprosy, psoriasis, and leprous ulcers
West African traditional medicine: leaves applied to wounds and skin infections
Traditional use as a longevity herb — legend that Sri Lankan elephants (long-lived) regularly eat gotu kola
modernUses: Wound healing: asiaticoside and madecassoside stimulate collagen synthesis and fibroblast proliferation; 8 RCTs support accelerated wound closure and reduced hypertrophic scarring
Cognitive enhancement: triterpene saponins increase BDNF, reduce acetylcholinesterase activity, and improve spatial memory in animal models and elderly clinical trials
Chronic venous insufficiency: meta-analysis of 12 RCTs shows reduced edema, improved capillary permeability, and reduced ankle swelling at 60–120 mg TTFCA/day
Anxiety reduction: brahmoside modulates GABA-A receptors; RCT shows reduced anxiety and improved mental alertness (vs. placebo)
Neuroprotection: madecassoside reduces amyloid-beta toxicity and protects against Parkinson's-like neurodegeneration in models
Stretch mark and scar prevention: topical formulations widely tested and commercially available with clinical support
Antioxidant: free radical scavenging and induction of glutathione and superoxide dismutase
Antidepressant: increased monoamine levels (5-HT, dopamine, norepinephrine) in stressed animal models
preparations: Fresh leaf consumption: 10–20 fresh leaves daily as food or blended into juice (Sri Lankan traditional dose)
Dried herb capsules: 500 mg–1 g twice daily
Standardized extract (TTFCA — total triterpenic fraction of Centella asiatica): 60 mg three times daily for chronic venous insufficiency (most clinically-studied form)
Tincture (1:5 in 30% ethanol): 3–5 mL twice daily
Standardized extract (8–10% asiaticosides): 300–600 mg twice daily for cognitive support
Topical cream (1% triterpene extract): applied twice daily to scars, stretch marks, and venous ulcers
contraindications: Pregnancy (high doses): emmenagogue activity at high doses; culinary amounts from food probably safe
Hepatic disease: rare case reports of hepatotoxicity with high-dose long-term use
Scheduled surgery: mild antiplatelet and anticoagulant effects; discontinue 2 weeks prior
Sedative drug use: mild GABA activity may potentiate CNS depression
Allergy to Apiaceae family plants
sideEffects: Nausea, stomach upset, and headache at high doses
Rare hepatotoxicity (case reports with very prolonged high-dose use)
Drowsiness and sedation at high therapeutic doses
Contact dermatitis with topical preparations (rare)
drugInteractions: Sedatives and anxiolytics (benzodiazepines, barbiturates): additive CNS depression from GABA activity
Anticoagulants and antiplatelets (warfarin, aspirin): mild antiplatelet effect; enhanced bleeding risk
Antidiabetic agents: mild blood glucose lowering; monitor for additive effect
Hepatotoxic drugs (isoniazid, acetaminophen, statins): rare hepatotoxicity risk is additive

name: Cramp Bark
nativeRange: Europe, northern Africa, and Central Asia; from the British Isles east to Siberia and south to the Caucasus and Iran; widely naturalized in North America as a garden escape
habitat: Moist woodland margins, hedgerows, riverbanks, and fen margins; grows on loamy, moderately fertile, neutral to slightly alkaline soils; partial shade to full sun; lowlands to 1,500 m elevation

description.overall appearance: A deciduous shrub or small tree growing 2–5 m tall with spreading branches, distinctive lobed leaves, and showy white flower clusters (resembling elderberry). The red berries are ornamental but toxic when raw.
description.roots: Woody, fibrous root system; roots not used medicinally; bark of roots and stems is the medicinal part
description.stem: Erect to spreading, multi-stemmed; bark gray-brown, shallowly fissured; young twigs slightly angular; opposite branching pattern
description.leaves: Opposite, 3-lobed (resembling maple), 5–10 cm long and wide; irregular coarsely toothed margins; upper surface dull green, lower surface hairier; petiole has 2 glands near blade; stunning red-orange autumn color
description.flowers: Creamy white, in flat-topped, lacecap-like corymbs 8–12 cm across; outer flowers sterile and enlarged (3–2.5 cm), inner flowers small and fertile; blooms May–June; fragrant
description.fruits/seeds: Bright red to orange, translucent, berry-like drupes 8–12 mm diameter; in drooping clusters; bitter, containing hydrocyanic acid precursors; single flattened stone per berry; persist into winter

compounds: Viopudial (sesquiterpene aldehyde, primary antispasmodic compound) — inhibits smooth muscle contraction
Viburnin (bitter resin mixture including valtratum and isovaltratum)
Scopoletin and scopolin (coumarins with antispasmodic and anti-inflammatory activity)
Oleanolic acid and ursolic acid (pentacyclic triterpenes)
Tannins: chlorogenic acid, caffeic acid, and condensed tannins
Valeric acid and isovaleric acid (sedative volatile acids)
partsUsed: Stem bark (primary medicinal part, harvested spring or autumn)
